title: Why Stop-Motion Animation Takes Forever to Make, With Nick Park | The Big Picture (Ep. 49)
format: Nick Park on why Early Man took five years, the Ray Harryhausen film that hooked him on animation, and running a 150-person stop-motion shoot across 35 sets.

films.title: One Million Years B.C.
films.note: The Ray Harryhausen film Park saw at 11 that made him pick up a camera and start animating dinosaurs out of clay.

films.title: Wallace & Gromit: The Curse of the Were-Rabbit
films.note: The last Aardman feature shot on film before the switch to digital cameras.

films.title: Early Man
films.note: Park's new film, a Stone Age-vs.-Bronze Age soccer comedy four or five years in the making, with its opening dinosaur fight built as a direct Harryhausen tribute.

films.title: The Nightmare Before Christmas
films.note: Henry Selick's film convinced Park and Aardman that stop-motion could carry a full feature.

films.title: A Grand Day Out
films.note: The first Wallace and Gromit short. Park animated about 90 percent of it himself.

films.title: The Wrong Trousers
films.note: Park and Steve Box handled all the animation, before the crew grew to seven and then to 35 or 40 on a feature.

films.title: Chicken Run
films.note: One of the features Park and producer Peter Lord built toward as Aardman expanded.

films.title: Coco
films.note: Park's answer to the show's closing question: the last great thing he'd seen.

films.title: Inside Out
films.note: Cited as proof Pete Docter can make something mature and entertaining at the same time.

films: A Matter of Loaf and Death

Wallace & Gromit: The Curse of the Were-.title: Wallace & Gromit: The Curse of the Were-Rabbit

A Matter of Loaf and Death.title: A Matter of Loaf and Death
